text: The patient is positioned supine with arms extended, and the surgical bed is slightly flexed. Cannulas one, two, and four are marked along the bikini line, while the working port arm three is positioned at the cephalad aspect of the umbilicus.
The surgeon uses a peri-umbilical optical trocar technique to gain access to the abdomen, followed by the administration of a laparoscopically guided transversus abdominis plane block.
The surgeon places the cannulas in their pre-planned positions along the bikini line, and the patient is positioned in twelve degrees of reverse Trendelenburg.
The robotic surgical setup includes a bipolar forceps in arm one, a hook cautery in arm three, a prograsp in arm four, and the camera positioned in arm two.
The surgeon retracts the gallbladder cephalad, revealing adhesions to the omentum.
The surgeon uses monopolar cautery to carefully dissect and take down adhesions, while maintaining awareness of the duodenum positioned below to prevent iatrogenic injury.
The surgeon continues the dissection near the duodenum, using cephalad retraction of the gallbladder to maintain a safe distance from the underlying duodenum.
The gallbladder is lowered towards the bowel table, initiating a domed-down technique, with visible manipulation of the gallbladder's position relative to surrounding structures.
The surgeon uses robotic instruments to grasp the gallbladder in a caudal direction while retracting the peritoneum of the gallbladder cephalad, facilitating exposure for further dissection.
The surgeon uses a hook cautery to incise the peritoneum of the gallbladder, developing a visible plane between the gallbladder and the liver.
The surgeon retracts the gallbladder cephalad, allowing the dissection of the closed triangle to proceed, with careful manipulation of the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon uses a hook cautery to dissect the gallbladder from the liver bed in a lateral to medial orientation, aiming to achieve a bloodless separation of the tissue planes.
The surgeon carefully dissects the gallbladder from the liver, ensuring that neither the liver nor the gallbladder is violated during the procedure.
Indocyanine green fluorescence imaging is used to visualize the gallbladder while preserving the integrity of the liver during the dissection.
The surgeon continues the dissection of the avascular plane along the neck of the gallbladder, using precise movements to separate it from the liver.
The surgeon carefully dissects adhesions around the neck of the gallbladder, taking care to avoid the duodenum during the procedure.
The surgeon employs a lateral to medial dissection technique, consistent with the critical view of safety, using hook cautery to separate the gallbladder from the liver bed in a bloodless plane.
The surgeon achieves significant freedom of the gallbladder on the lateral dimension before proceeding to dissect the medial side, ensuring careful manipulation of the gallbladder.
The surgeon identifies the cystic artery and cystic duct using indocyanine green fluorescence imaging, which highlights these structures for precise visualization.
The surgeon creates a window between the cystic duct and cystic artery, dissecting the cystic structures to ensure clear identification of these anatomical features.

The surgeon dissects the gallbladder away from the liver in the medial plane, achieving separation of approximately one third of its distance, providing an adequate view for the critical view of safety.
The surgeon employs a doublet technique to confirm that the cystic duct and cystic artery are the only two structures entering the gallbladder, ensuring precise identification and isolation.
The surgeon clips the cystic artery twice and the cystic duct three times, with an additional clip placed on the gallbladder side, before transecting the cystic duct.
The surgeon transects the cystic artery using an electrocautery device.
The surgeon uses hook electrocautery to remove the gallbladder from the liver, carefully maintaining the avascular plane between the gallbladder and liver tissue.
The surgeon performs a bloodless dissection of the gallbladder from the liver, maintaining the avascular plane between the two structures.
The surgeon carefully removes the gallbladder from the liver, ensuring that neither the gallbladder nor the liver is violated during the dissection process.
After the complete removal of the gallbladder from the liver, the surgeon inserts an endocatch bag percutaneously and deposits the gallbladder into it.
The surgeon removes the intact gallbladder from the abdomen using an endocatch bag, ensuring no spillage of biliary contents.
The surgeon closes the extraction site using a transvascular suture passer, applying a figure of eight suture technique.
The video shows the cosmetic outcome of the surgical site after the gallbladder removal, highlighting the absence of visible scarring or tissue damage.
